projects.title: Impostor Game
projects.description: Instant-play party game. No downloads needed! Open the browser and start playing with friends. Available in multiple languages (ES/EN/FR/ZH) with automatic word translation. Simple social deduction mechanics with voting system and swipe-to-reveal roles. Works offline as PWA.

details.summary: No-download party game. Browser-based, multiple languages, instant play.
content: Ever tried getting everyone to download the same game app at a party? Forget that. 🎉
This is a party game that lives in your browser. Share the link and you're ready in 5 seconds. No app stores, no 'sorry I don't have space,' no waiting.
Why it works for everyone:
<ul>
• Zero friction: Just open a link, no downloads
• Truly global: Spanish, English, French, Chinese... with live word translation
• Quick setup: Configure players, time, and rules in seconds
• Works everywhere: Any mobile browser, install as PWA or stay lightweight

The gameplay? Classic social deduction: everyone except the impostors gets the same secret word (like 'Beach' or 'Pizza'). Through careful questioning and voting, figure out who's faking it. Swipe to reveal roles, vote for suspects, and watch the chaos unfold.
It's your party, your rules.

diagram.code:
flowchart TD
    Start[Start Screen] --> Theme[Theme Selection]
    Theme --> Setup[Player Setup]
    Setup --> Reveal[Role Reveal]
    Reveal --> Game[Game Screen]
    Game --> Vote{Voting System}
    Vote -->|Correct| Check{All Found?}
    Vote -->|Wrong| Game
    Check -->|No| Game
    Check -->|Yes| End[Game Over]
    Game -->|Manual Reveal| End
    End --> Start

projects.title: JobHub
projects.description: Stop browsing job sites page by page. JobHub crawls postings for you, gives you one place to search and filter them, and tracks every application so you always know where you stand. Built as a real-world exercise in designing multi-service architecture beyond the usual POCs.

details.summary: One place to find, filter, and track job applications — so you can focus on landing the job, not managing spreadsheets.
content: Job hunting shouldn't feel like a second job.

You know the drill: open LinkedIn, scroll through pages, check another job board, copy a link into a spreadsheet, lose track of what you applied to last week. It's exhausting and it adds up fast when you're already in a stressful situation.
JobHub takes that off your plate.
What it does:
<ul>
• Crawls job postings automatically — no more going site by site
• Search and filter from a single interface with saved presets, so you don't repeat the same searches every day
• Track every application with a timeline: applied, interviewing, offer, rejected — always know where each one stands
• Dashboard with stats so you can see your progress at a glance instead of guessing
Why I built it:

After building games and small proof-of-concepts, I wanted to tackle something with real architectural complexity — multiple services with clear boundaries, a shared database with proper isolation, user authentication, and a frontend that ties it all together. I used GenAI to speed up implementation so I could focus on the architecture and functional design rather than boilerplate.
The technical details (service breakdown, API contracts, deployment) are all in the docs.
A tool born from the frustration of job hunting, and the ambition to build something more complex than what I'd done before.

diagram.code:
graph LR UI["JobHub-ui
(React + Vite)"] -->|/auth| Auth["auth-service
(Quarkus :8082)"] UI -->|/jobs| Job["job-service
(Quarkus :8081)"] UI -->|/applications| App["application-service
(Quarkus :8083)"] Crawler["crawler-service
(scheduled)"] --> DB App -->|snapshot| Job App -->|verify| Auth Auth --> DB[("PostgreSQL
schema-per-service")] Job --> DB App --> DB

projects.title: Pay & Go
projects.description: Event-driven payment microservices platform built with Go. Learning project exploring Clean Architecture, Domain-Driven Design, and real-time event streaming with Kafka. Features account and card management services with asynchronous communication.

details.summary: Microservices with Go, Kafka, and Clean Architecture principles.
content: Learning Go by building something real.

This was my deep dive into Go, microservices architecture, and event-driven systems. Two services (Account & Card) communicate asynchronously through Kafka, following Clean Architecture principles to the letter.
What makes it interesting:
<ul>
• True microservices: Separate services with clear boundaries, loosely coupled via Kafka events
• Clean Architecture: Domain entities know nothing about JSON or databases. Pure business logic all the way down
• Event-driven flow: Create an account → Kafka carries the event → Card service syncs automatically. No direct API calls
• Production-ready patterns: Health checks, containerization with Podman, Kubernetes manifests, one-command deployment
• Test coverage: 100% on domain & application layers. Integration tests for the full event flow
Interactive UI included: Open ui.html in a browser to create accounts, issue cards, and watch Kafka events flow in real-time.
Built as a proof of concept to learn Go and distributed systems patterns. Turns out, microservices are just as messy (and fun) as everyone says.

diagram.code:
graph LR
    UI[Web UI] -->|HTTP| AS[Account Service]
    UI -->|HTTP| CS[Card Service]
    AS -->|Publish Events| K[Kafka]
    K -->|Consume Events| CS
    AS --> AR[(In-Memory Repo)]
    CS --> CR[(In-Memory Repo)]
    
    style K fill:#ff6b6b
    style AS fill:#4ecdc4
    style CS fill:#45b7d1

config.blurb: Building full-stack apps, APIs and data pipelines — Java, Go, Python and whatever else gets the job done.
